proposition: Evidence of prior settlement or verdict amounts is irrelevant to liability and damages in a subsequent, unrelated accident
cited_case: Tompkins v. Cyr, 202 F.3d 770, 787 (5th Cir. 2000)
supporting_language: The opinion does not discuss prior settlement or verdict amounts, nor their relevance to subsequent unrelated accidents. The case involves harassment by anti-abortion protesters — nothing about excluding prior settlement evidence. The quote "consequential fact" is FABRICATED and does not appear in the opinion.

proposition: Evidence that had no bearing on the specific issues before the jury should be excluded
cited_case: Collins v. Wayne Corp., 621 F.2d 777, 784 (5th Cir. 1980)
retrieved_case: H. E. Collins and Joetta Collins, Surviving Parents and Personal Representatives of Karen Collins, Deceased v. Wayne Corporation
supporting_language: The opinion addresses relevance on cross-examination, but its holding is the opposite of what the brief implies: the court held that "strict rules of relevancy are relaxed on cross-examination" and upheld admission of prior testimony to impeach credibility, even though it involved "an entirely different subject." The brief cites this for excluding irrelevant evidence, but the opinion actually favored admission.

proposition: Rule 403 grants trial courts wide discretion to exclude evidence where the risk of prejudice outweighs probative value
cited_case: United States v. McRae, 593 F.2d 700, 707 (5th Cir. 1979)
retrieved_case: United States v. Stephen Roderick McRae
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "the application of Rule 403 must be cautious and sparing. Its major function is limited to excluding matter of scant or cumulative probative force." (p. 707) Quote "wide discretion" is VERBATIM.

proposition: Unfair prejudice under Rule 403 refers to evidence that has an undue tendency to suggest decision on an improper basis
cited_case: Old Chief v. United States, 519 U.S. 172, 180 (1997)
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "'Unfair prejudice' within its context means an undue tendency to suggest decision on an improper basis, commonly, though not necessarily, an emotional one." (p. 180) Both quotes VERBATIM.

proposition: The Collateral Source Rule is of common law origin and bars a tortfeasor from reducing their liability because the plaintiff received compensation from an independent source
cited_case: Bozeman v. State, 879 So. 2d 692, 698 (La. 2004)
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "The collateral source rule is a rule of evidence and damages that is of common law origin" and "a tortfeasor may not benefit, and an injured plaintiff's tort recovery may not be reduced, because of monies received by the plaintiff from sources independent of the tortfeasor's procuration or contribution." (pp. 697-698) Quote VERBATIM.

proposition: Spoliation sanctions require proof that the party acted in bad faith; mere negligence is insufficient
cited_case: King v. Illinois Cent. R.R., 337 F.3d 550, 556 (5th Cir. 2003)
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "An adverse inference based on the destruction of potential evidence is predicated on the 'bad conduct' of the defendant" and "King must show that ICR acted in 'bad faith.'" (p. 556)

proposition: Spoliation sanctions require proof that the party acted in bad faith; mere negligence is insufficient
cited_case: Condrey v. SunTrust Bank of Ga., 431 F.3d 191, 203 (5th Cir. 2005)
retrieved_case: Condrey v. Suntrust Bank of GA
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "The Fifth Circuit permits an adverse inference against the destroyer of evidence only upon a showing of 'bad faith' or 'bad conduct.'" (pp. 408-411)

proposition: Spoliation sanctions require proof that the party acted in bad faith; mere negligence is insufficient
cited_case: Guzman v. Jones, 804 F.3d 707, 713 (5th Cir. 2015)
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "We permit an adverse inference against the spoliator or sanctions against the spoliator only upon a showing of 'bad faith' or 'bad conduct.'" Bad faith defined as "destruction for the purpose of hiding adverse evidence."

proposition: The Supreme Court has long recognized the prejudicial impact of arrest evidence where no conviction exists
cited_case: Michelson v. United States, 335 U.S. 469, 475-76 (1948)
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "Arrest without more does not, in law any more than in reason, impeach the integrity or impair the credibility of a witness. It happens to the innocent as well as the guilty." The Court further noted the jury "almost surely cannot comprehend the judge's limiting instruction," recognizing the prejudicial impact. (pp. 475-76, 482-485)

proposition: Evidence must be excluded under Rule 403 when any minimal probative value is substantially outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice
cited_case: United States v. McRae, 593 F.2d 700, 707 (5th Cir. 1979)
retrieved_case: United States v. Stephen Roderick McRae
supporting_language: The opinion discusses Rule 403's framework but does not state evidence "must be excluded" when "any minimal probative value" is outweighed. The opinion emphasizes discretion: "Rule 403 is meant to relax the iron rule of relevance, to permit the trial judge to preserve the fairness of the proceedings by exclusion." The proposition overstates by using mandatory language and lowering the threshold.

proposition: Bias evidence must demonstrate actual bias affecting the witness's testimony
cited_case: United States v. Abel, 469 U.S. 45, 52 (1984)
supporting_language: Abel defines bias as "the relationship between a party and a witness which might lead the witness to slant, unconsciously or otherwise, his testimony" but allows bias to be inferred from circumstances (shared gang membership) — it does not require proof of "actual bias affecting the witness's testimony" as the proposition states. The proposition overstates the standard.

proposition: The eggshell plaintiff doctrine holds that a tortfeasor takes the victim as they find them
cited_case: Maurer v. United States, 668 F.2d 98, 99 (2d Cir. 1981)
retrieved_case: Louis A. Maurer v. United States
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "It is a settled principle of tort law that when a defendant's wrongful act causes injury, he is fully liable for the resulting damage even though the injured plaintiff had a preexisting condition that made the consequences of the wrongful act more severe than they would have been for a normal victim. The defendant takes the plaintiff as he finds him." (p. 99)

proposition: Defendants are liable for all consequences of their negligence even when those consequences are more severe due to the plaintiff's pre-existing vulnerabilities
cited_case: Koch v. United States, 857 F.3d 267, 279 (5th Cir. 2017)
supporting_language: (1) Supports: "when a defendant's negligent act causes injury to someone with a preexisting condition, the defendant is fully liable for all resulting damage" and "a defendant takes the plaintiff as he finds him."
